food_subgroup: Chilled sago-based dessert with coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for sago pudding; closest parent is pudding food product (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers sago as a starch ingredient; no specific prepared sago pudding entry exists. Used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for sago pudding as a prepared dessert food.

preparation_method: sweetened with sugar or palm sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sago pudding is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small dessert bowl as typically served at Singaporean coffee shops and dessert stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Sago pudding with coconut milk — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central sago starch reference, adapted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists sago-based desserts including sago pudding with coconut milk. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo entry for sago dessert with coconut milk and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169717, tapioca/sago starch) for starch macros. Coconut milk contribution estimated from HPB coconut milk entry. Composite per-100g values reflect a typical preparation of approximately 60% sago pearls cooked, 35% coconut milk, 5% sugar.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sago pearls have a reported GI of approximately 70 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists sago as a high-GI starch. The GI of the prepared sago pudding with coconut milk is estimated at 70, consistent with the dominant sago starch component. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (70 × 22.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 23.6 g.

hypertension.guidance: Sago pudding contains approximately 18 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium dessert option suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided added salt or high-sodium toppings are avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and significant carbohydrate load from sago starch
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sago pudding has a high GI of approximately 70 and delivers approximately 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a glycemic load of ~23.6 per standard serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and pair with low-GI foods to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk component contributes approximately 3.7 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume sago pudding in moderation and be mindful of cumulative saturated fat intake from coconut-based desserts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sago pudding (also known as Sago Sago in the Singaporean café context) is a classic Southeast Asian chilled dessert made from small translucent sago pearls cooked in water until soft and gelatinous, then combined with rich coconut milk and sweetened with sugar or palm sugar. The dish is commonly flavoured with pandan leaf, which imparts a subtle grassy, vanilla-like aroma. The texture is soft, slippery, and lightly chewy, with a creamy, mildly sweet coconut flavour. In Singapore, sago pudding is a staple dessert at traditional coffee shops (kopitiams), hawker centres, and heritage-style cafés such as Sinpopo Coffee. It is often served chilled in small bowls or cups, sometimes garnished with coconut cream, gula melaka (palm sugar syrup), or coloured jelly. Nutritionally, sago pudding is predominantly a carbohydrate-dense dessert with moderate fat from coconut milk and very low protein. The sago starch has a high glycemic index (~70), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content from coconut milk warrants moderation for those managing cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is low, posing minimal concern for hypertension. Sago pearls are derived from the pith of the sago palm (Metroxylon sagu) or from tapioca starch, and are naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and vegan.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sago pudding?
What is sago sago at Sinpopo Coffee?

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo lists sago-based desserts and coconut milk desserts for Singapore; nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central sago starch reference as no single exact entry for this specific Sinpopo Coffee preparation exists. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Food name normalised from 'Sago Sago' (menu label at Sinpopo Coffee - Funan) to canonical English name 'Sago Pudding'. The menu description '2 pieces for $8' suggests individual portions; serving size set at 150 g per portion as a standard dessert bowl estimate. Vitamin A is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA sago starch source entries. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Folate is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero as sago pudding contains no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin D is confirmed zero as sago pudding contains no vitamin D sources. Trans fat is confirmed zero per the plant-based ingredient profile. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dessert. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 22.5 × 1.50) / 100 = 23.6. All -1 sentinel values reflect nutrients not reported in the cited approved sources.
